key_questions: Typical symptoms: retrosternal burning, regurgitation, worsened post-prandially/lying down?
Alarm symptoms (dysphagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, vomiting)?
Age >50 with new-onset symptoms (increased risk of Barrett's/cancer)?
Current medications worsening GERD (CCBs, nitrates, TCAs, bisphosphonates)?
red_flags: Dysphagia or odynophagia → structural pathology — endoscopy
Unintentional weight loss + GI symptoms → malignancy
GI bleeding (hematemesis, melena) → upper GI bleed — ER
Age >50 with new-onset symptoms → endoscopy to exclude Barrett's oesophagus/cancer

first_line.name: Proton Pump Inhibitor
first_line.drugs: Omeprazole (Losec) 20 mg OD 30–60 min before breakfast × 4–8 weeks | Pantoprazole (Pantoloc) 20–40 mg OD before breakfast
first_line.notes: Most effective acid suppression. Take 30–60 min BEFORE breakfast — proton pumps must be active for PPIs to work. Step down to lowest effective dose after 4–8 weeks. For on-demand GERD: omeprazole 20 mg when symptoms occur. Long-term: annual review of indication; step-down if possible.

second_line.name: H2 Receptor Antagonist
second_line.notes: Faster onset than PPIs (30 min vs 1–4h) — useful for breakthrough or PRN. Less effective than PPI for severe or frequent GERD. Tachyphylaxis develops with regular use. Can use famotidine at bedtime + morning PPI for nocturnal breakthrough.

second_line.name: Antacids
second_line.drugs: Calcium carbonate (Tums) | Aluminum/magnesium hydroxide (Gaviscon, Maalox) PRN
second_line.notes: Immediate relief (minutes) but short duration (30–60 min). PRN for mild breakthrough. Gaviscon: forms raft over stomach contents — useful for regurgitation. Not for regular scheduled use.

non_pharm: Head of bed elevation 15–20 cm (for nocturnal symptoms — most effective lifestyle intervention)
Lose weight if overweight (most evidence-based lifestyle intervention)
Avoid trigger foods (individualized): coffee, citrus, tomatoes, spicy foods, high-fat meals, chocolate, mint
Small frequent meals rather than large meals
treatment.refer_when: Alarm symptoms (dysphagia, weight loss, bleeding); age >50 with new symptoms; failure to respond to PPI × 4–8 weeks; suspected atypical GERD (cough, hoarseness, asthma); recurrent GERD requiring ongoing PPI
